title: Rosemary focaccia
subtitle: Olive oil-drenched flatbread dimpled with fresh rosemary and sea salt
excerpt: Focaccia: a high-hydration bread dough stretched into a generously oiled tin, dimpled with fingers, scattered with rosemary and flakes of sea salt, and baked until the crust is blistered and gold. The bottom is oily and crisp; the interior is open and airy.
sourceNotes: Focaccia is an Italian flatbread documented from Roman times, when panis focacius referred to bread baked on the hearth. The modern Ligurian form, enriched with olive oil and topped with herbs and salt, is documented in Italian domestic cookery from the nineteenth century. Ada Boni, Il Talismano della Felicità (1925), and Marcella Hazan, Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking (1992), document the canonical Ligurian rosemary and salt version. The recipe as given is fully public-domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions. Reheat at 180°C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough benefits from a cold overnight prove in the fridge. Dimple, top, and bake the next day.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven, upper-middle shelf, very hot

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, 220°C, 22 to 25 minutes until deeply golden with blistered crust

glossaryTerms.definition: Dimpling focaccia with your fingers just before baking serves two purposes. The holes help the olive oil pool and collect, so the surface doesn't dry out during baking. They also interrupt the surface tension and prevent the focaccia from puffing up into a dome rather than staying flat. Press straight down to the base of the tin, not at an angle. The dough is very wet at this stage and the dimples will partly close, which is fine — they re-open in the oven as the bread sets. Dimple generously: 30 to 40 holes for a standard tin.

content.text: Focaccia is the most forgiving bread you can bake. High hydration, minimal shaping, no scoring. The dough goes directly from the bowl into an oiled tin. The overnight cold prove is not essential but it significantly improves the flavour and the open, airy crumb.
Mix, bulk ferment, cold prove overnight

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-dried}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, 60 ml of the {{olive-oil}}, and 390 ml cool water in a large bowl. Mix until no dry flour remains. The dough will be very wet and shaggy. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes, folding the dough from the edges into the centre every 20 minutes (4 sets of folds in total). Transfer to the fridge and cold-prove overnight.
Stretch into the tin, rest 1 hour

Pour the remaining {{olive-oil}} into a 30 × 20 cm baking tin and spread to coat. Tip the cold dough into the tin and gently stretch it toward the corners. If it springs back, leave it for 10 minutes and try again. Leave to warm and puff for 1 hour.
Preheat the oven to 220°C fan. See the

: press your fingers all the way to the base of the tin in 30 to 40 places. Scatter the {{rosemary}} leaves and {{sea-salt-flakes}} evenly. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until deeply golden. Cool for 15 minutes in the tin before lifting out.
